content:
When you strip the standard library from your Rust project, the entire error handling paradigm shifts from runtime polymorphism to compile-time structural guarantees. Instead of wrapping heterogeneous error types in a `Box<dyn std::error::Error>`, which forces dynamic dispatch and prevents compile-time type checking on the underlying data, you embrace the static nature of `core`. The idiomatic approach is to define your own custom error enum that implements `core::fmt::Display` directly. For instance, if you are building a simple filesystem abstraction in an embedded context without heap allocation, you might declare `pub enum MyError { FileNotFound, PermissionDenied, InvalidPath }`, ensuring each variant carries the specific information needed for recovery without needing to box an unknown trait object. This eliminates the overhead of dynamic dispatch entirely because the compiler knows exactly which variant is active at compile time, allowing you to pattern match on errors with perfect type safety and zero runtime indirection.

Consider a scenario where you are writing a network stack for a microcontroller; instead of returning `Result<(), Box<dyn Error>>`, your function signature becomes `Result<(), MyError>`. When the network layer fails due to a packet loss, it returns `Err(MyError::PacketLoss)`, and the caller can immediately inspect that specific variant rather than trying to downcast or debug an opaque trait object. This is particularly crucial for embedded systems where memory is scarce; avoiding heap allocation via `Box` means your error objects fit directly on the stack or within registers, drastically reducing the binary size of your firmware. Furthermore, when you need even more efficiency, you can leverage compile-time strings using `core::str` and `core::fmt::Display`, which allows error messages to be baked into the compilation process itself rather than being stored as runtime data structures. If you are targeting a resource-constrained device like an ARM Cortex-M4, you might further optimize by integrating `defmt` logging from `embedded-hal`. The `defmt` library generates debug output at compile time using a custom format string parser, meaning your error logs become static text in the final binary with no runtime overhead whatsoever. This is ideal for boot-up diagnostics where every byte counts and you cannot afford to log heap-allocated strings or perform expensive dynamic dispatch operations.

You must also understand when to gate these optimizations using `cfg(feature = "std")`. If your codebase needs the full richness of `std::error::Error`—such as chaining errors, implementing `From`, or utilizing backtraces—you should wrap your error types in a conditional compilation block that activates only when the standard library is present. For example, you can define an internal trait or enum that uses `Option<Box<dyn std::error::Error>>` inside a feature gate, ensuring that your core logic remains lean and portable across targets while still offering advanced debugging capabilities on desktop or server environments where heap memory is abundant. This modular approach gives you the flexibility to write one codebase that behaves like a high-performance embedded application on chips but gracefully degrades into a standard Rust library when compiled with `std` enabled, giving developers the best of both worlds depending on their deployment context.

The most common pitfall when migrating to a no_std environment is the naive assumption that you can simply replace std::error::Error with core::fmt::Display and expect it to behave identically. This approach fails because the Error trait relies on dynamic dispatch via the dyn keyword, which requires the standard library's type system and heap allocation capabilities that are absent in pure no_std contexts. When you attempt to return an Option<Box<dyn Error>> or Result<T, Box<dyn Error>> without the std feature enabled, the compiler rejects the code because it cannot construct a trait object of unknown size at runtime; instead, you must use static types with known memory layouts. The correct progression begins with recognizing that no_std environments—such as bare metal embedded systems or WASI targets—do not have garbage collection or dynamic type erasure available to them. Instead of using the Error trait which demands a vtable and heap allocation, you should adopt compile-time string errors by implementing core::fmt::Display for your custom error types directly on the enum variants themselves. For instance, defining an enum like Result<T, MyError> where MyError implements Display allows you to propagate failures without any runtime overhead or boxing. This strategy scales well when combined with defmt, a low-level debug logging framework specifically designed for resource-constrained embedded systems where memory is scarce and execution cycles are limited. Defmt provides a deterministic way to serialize error values into a byte stream that can be captured by an RTOS or a hardware debugger without triggering exceptions or requiring heap fragments. You should only gate richer error handling behind feature = 'std' using cfg-if macros when your application requires full diagnostic messages, stack traces, or the ability to format errors with human-readable text at runtime. This conditional compilation ensures that no_std builds remain lightweight and predictable while still offering the convenience of standard library features when explicitly requested. The key insight is that error handling in no_std must be explicit about its assumptions: either you accept static, compile-time checked strings via Display or static enums with defmt serialization, never dynamic trait objects unless std is actively enabled.

The most critical lesson in no_std error handling is that you cannot rely on the dynamic dispatch overhead of Box<dyn Trait> or the heap allocation inherent to std::error::Error, which forces every panic path into a stack trace and consumes precious RAM on constrained devices. Instead, you must embrace compile-time string errors using enums like Result<T, MyError> where MyError is an enum variant containing static strings such as InvalidInput or OutOfBounds, ensuring the error type is known at compile time and zero-sized when no data is needed, or minimal when it contains a small constant vector. You will also frequently encounter core::fmt::Display implementations required to convert these enums into human-readable messages for logging without triggering unwinding costs that slow down real-time control loops in embedded systems where every nanosecond counts. For high-frequency debugging scenarios on microcontrollers lacking an RTOS, defmt is the superior choice because it emits a single integer per error message via hardware debug pins rather than writing to volatile memory buffers or blocking execution with string formatting operations, allowing you to stream thousands of events per second without impacting application latency. Finally, always gate your custom rich error types with feature = 'std' so that when std is disabled, the code falls back to lightweight enum variants and core::fmt::Display traits automatically, preventing compilation failures or runtime panics in bare-metal environments where dynamic allocation and exception handling are forbidden by design.
